sentences.text: I'll be right back, Schneck.
Okay.
There's our big boy.
Hey, hey!
How was… how was Spring of Sprags?
It was good.
I just… I love, … I actually really like it, because the yoga is just so… it always feels good when you do some yoga.
Yoga is really nice.
Is this when you wait these two calls, Alec?
You throw a collar on for this one?
Yeah, it's really all about what the customer wants.
Fair, fair, fair.
I think Gavin wanted the black tea.
And again, G wants the caller, so, you know, I'm a vessel.
for our customers' preferences, Corey.
Thanks for joining, that was good.
Yeah, I think that was a good one.
So, Corey, you have the background on… yeah.
I know nothing about this, I wasn't on the phone, so I don't….
Yeah, I can't find any notes in granola for our last call.
I sent the notes from May.
Yeah, I was just trying to find those.
Was it… Nope.
We had granola going, or at least I didn't for that call.
Oh, okay.
I have the detailed notes in product board.
Okay.
Lyle.
And then, who's the ownership group, Corey, that's, ….
Ani, right?
Oso, or Ano, Oso, or Oso, or I'm not sure how you say it.
And they were doing two MG properties, right?
We weren't sure if MG was aware that was the case.
They're doing one.
Okay.
Now, we don't have… here's the… did you find the link, Alec, or do you want it here?
I haven't found it yet now.
There, I just sent it in the chat.
This was back in May 23rd.
And so, Schneck, just in terms of agenda for this call.
What's this….
This was….
I'll be in on this one.
Megan was like, we need to re-engage them, so this is just structured as, like, a product update meeting.
You can cover both Explore and Daylight.
I think I can run intra, like, I don't really… we've all… this group has all met at different times before.

So I don't think we need to do intros, but it is, like, Chris Hagen and some, … And, like, development acquisitions analyst, and then it's also the revenue management team, so both products will be… be relevant.
Okay, got it.
There's 5 in the waiting room.
I also think we can ask Charlotta how she wants to use the time.
She wants us to focus on one or the other.
Okay, I'll be a fly on the wall to a degree.
Appreciate it.
I'll set it at 110%.
Morning!
Morning!
Is it… it's not morning for you, is it?
No, it's night.
Afternoon, 6pm.
day.
I do that nearly every time.
How are you doing?
Good, how are you?
I've been… I've been well, yeah.
Good.
I've been well.
It's good to see everybody.
Maddie, you gotta tell her about the class you took last night?
I took Spanga, which is a mixture of spin, a HIIT workout, and yoga.
So….
Spinning, HIIT, and yoga, all in one?
Little 60-minute fitness buffet.
I have never heard of that.
I feel like Chris could get into that a little bit, I don't know.
I'm not that old yet.
I think I spooked Chris last time I saw him.
I was like, that's Chris, I jumped up on him at Las Vegas.
Interrupted this conversation, so….
You're the heck out of me.
Yeah.
How you doing, Troy?
I'm doing well, I'm doing well.
Not… not as active as you, it sounds like, but I'm doing well.
Yeah, let's talk about that a few more minutes.
No, Troy, I….
of that.
I barely slept, I'm slamming Diet Coke here, what am I doing wrong?
Come on, I gotta live healthier like Matthew here.
Well, good to see you all.
We've got some pretty awesome product updates.
I don't know, Charlotte, I kind of wanted to rely on you to see what you want us to focus on more here.
So, relevant updates to the revenue management tool, since the last time we met, it's more fully built out.
We are piloting with some clients of ours, at the moment, and then Explorer has had some major updates as well.
Yeah, if you want to leave revenue management till the end, we have quite a few groups from our acquisitions department, and then they can jump off.
When you've gone through the market analysis part.
Okay, great.

And, I don't know, Corey, maybe if you want to jump in here and, or Charlotta, or Chris, if you want to give some background just on current processes and how you all think about, growth at MG and, current tools that you're using.
Charlotte, do you wanna, … I'm not sure I heard of that last part, Matthew.
Yeah, I'm just… Explore is going to be functionally, broader market dynamics, supply information, demographic, transactions, and so, just wondering where you all are accessing that data, today.
Well, I think from the acquisition standpoint, they probably just want to, you know, analyze, potentially new markets or new states even, and see what kind of information is readily available.
Certainly from pricing and… You know, any operating statistics you'd have?
Generally, they're pulling from everything, every source possible, gathering information from multiple sources.
But I'll let Paul and Jake and you guys comment on that.
Yeah, I'd say, I mean, obviously we're big Yardi users, so we use everything we can there, and all the pricing data that we have right now, … you know, we try to incorporate that as well.
But like anything, we underwrite just like everyone else does.
We want to under… understand what the pipeline is like, we want to understand what rents are like at all the comps, and we're just… You know, doing all the typical financial analysis.
So, looking forward to understanding kind of what this tool has that would assist us in doing that better.
And what I'd add is, part of our… part of our process has been, using AIQ, using the research function.
For rent comp surveys, using that to pull in information on concessions and net effective pricing.

And so, you know, to the extent that there are improvements in that area, either to the data collection, I know, you know, something that's very important to us, and not always the easiest to get information on is you know, what concessions are being offered at different properties, on what unit types, etc.
And then, you know, to the extent that there are different levels of renovations at different properties, it's helpful to know when we're, you know, looking at unit data.
Are we looking at renovated units, are we looking at unrenovated units, etc.
So… Those are kind of the… those are kind of the main data points that we're using now for MayIQ, and obviously any additional information that could be useful, you know, I'd love to know about it.
We can speak to that, Jake, and maybe between the comments that Paul also shared as well, I would be curious where you're finding inefficiencies within your workflows as you're underwriting.
I'd love to understand that, and then hopefully we can show you how we could help.
Yeah, I mean, I….
Go ahead, Jake.
Yeah, I mean, I think, … I think, you know, one of the… One of the most, kind of, time-intensive.

Parts of underwriting is doing, you know, rent comp surveys that are detailed enough To, you know, really give us the information we're looking for, so… You know, we want to be looking at, units at comparable properties that have comparable renovation types, what kind of concessions are they offering, … You know, renovation scopes, location information, … etc.
You know, how do the amenities compare?
How do the, you know, other income kind of fee structures compare?
What are they using for their… their pricing software?
Are they on, you know, revenue management or not?

So… just that kind of research process tends to take… tends to be pretty, you know, time-intensive, and we certainly have found some efficiencies in… in using AIQ to help us gather that data, but, you know, there certainly is still some, Some kind of manual… Processes in terms of cleaning up the data and, you know, understanding the properties themselves a little bit better, you know, qualitatively.

So, to Troy's point, using all of the data sources to pull all of that together, ApartmentIQ included, and then If we could assume, and manually maybe even going to property websites to fill in the blanks.
Correct, yeah.
One thing… couple things I would add is future rent growth.
That's all current information, but one of the issues, and it's not an issue, it's a discussion between acquisition, asset management, and operations, is what does future rent growth look like?
Yeah.
And you pull from different sources, and everyone's got different percent rent growth.
And so, what do you use?
Do you… kind of take it all in and just still make your educated guesstimate as to what that rent growth would be.
So, future looking, you know, some tool that would allow us to look and say, okay, you're expecting 3% rent growth next year, or 5%, or 1%, whatever it may be.
I like, on… on current data for… I call them rent rankings, where you… it would be great if you would have the ability to adjust rents down to an apples-to-apples basis.
And when I say adjust, meaning You know, adjust for age of a property, adjust for amenities, adjust for location, adjust for the size of the units.
You know, simple adjustments like, does this unit have a washer-dryer and this unit doesn't?

You know, if there were a I'm just thinking of this right now, but if there were an ability to go in and say, okay, this property has washer-dryers, we need to… and our property that we're looking at doesn't, we need to drop those rents by $75.
kind of put everything on an apples-to-apples basis, and then see how your rents compare.
Because there should always be some difference in rents.
You know, you have a new property next door versus a 30-year-old property.
you know, they're… their same unit size shouldn't have necessarily the same rents.
There should be some adjustment factor there.

And if there were some tool that would, you know, allow us to make some adjustments like that, make the adjustments, and then see how they… they sit, and whether they're aligned at that point, I think that would be a pretty cool tool.
I would also like to rank the comps.

Basically, we would give them a ranking, so we would say, this is our number 1 competitor, they get a 1, this is our number 2 competitor, just so that we really know who's the most relevant comp, because sometimes we have, you know, 10, 15 comps that we want to track, but we really, We kind of have to keep track of who was… who was the most relevant comp, and who are we just adding to the survey to kind of track and see what they're doing.
I tried not to smile, Alec.
We've had this request, before, Charlotte, and I think it's… It's nearly live, so we….
Okay, good.
I think I've requested it before as well, so I'm glad, glad to hear that.
It's a budding product manager.
I don't like trade-offs, so he's not a big fan of trade-offs.
Yeah, I think we'll have great updates for a number of the items.
That you all have highlighted.
Anything else that would be worth covering?
No, obviously these are all wish lists, you know, and obviously it takes time and all that, so….
Well, I think, I think it's… dead on just about everything that we are shipping or, are planning to in the near future.
So, … I think we could provide some good updates.
Okay.
Okay Do you want me to dive in?
Corey, or anything else?
I think so, yeah, I think, why don't we show the team what we're talking about, and to Alec's point.
We ask your feedback because we incorporate it, so please, share the good and the bad.
We'd love to integrate your responses into our roadmap if possible, but hopefully you like what you see.
Okay, perfect.
Charlotta, as Corey mentioned, we are building the custom comp ranking as we speak.
So, we should have a good update for you in the next week or two, and we'll be sure to ping you and let you know, Larissa, as we get that out.
We also, Troy, you mentioned future rent growth.
We just deployed, we've got a… … We just had our future rent growth forecasts just go live in our new Explore product as well.
So, we're doing that at the MSA level.
That's going to be about across 150 MSAs by early next week, and then we're gonna go down to the city and zip level as well.
And I'll talk a little bit about what our approach has been there, and whether or not, that fits into how you're thinking about things.
Amenity premiums.
Jake, I hear you on understanding the renovated premiums, and then also, Troy, you mentioned amenity premiums, you know, what is a washer-dryer worth?
How much are folks achieving on renovations?
These are our requests that we're getting very consistently as well, and that's work that we're planning to undertake.
This quarter.
So, we've got a data team, actually, that's pretty focused on this.

Right now, you can, in the product… the product right now uses large language models to look at floor plan names, and then tag amenities, looking at things like… If it's listed in the unit, if it's in images, etc.
We think to really get to really good coverage, though, we're gonna have to do some more data science, where we're really comparing apples to apples units that have a premium Built into them.
So, we'll talk a little bit more about that, but Jake, to that extent, if you're… if you're up for it, we'd love to be able to run these new models by you as we're deploying them.
It should be in the near future here.
On renovated units, for sure, and then I think on amenity premiums as well.
I would love to involve you, Troy, and give you updates along the way.
Yep, great.
Yeah, absolutely.
Great.
Okay, perfect.
So why don't I… I'll jump in and show you all what's new today.
And then we can take it from there.
So, sounds like you all are getting some use out of helping, using IQ to automate your rent comp process.

So, you're familiar with research, which enables you to create a comp survey for any property in the country that's existing, or if you want to create a custom property as well for new development, you can do that very easily.
… So… Hopefully you're familiar with that.
And then what I want to show you today, and so here's the, the interface.
We did update the interface with a few items recently.
So we have pulled out the metrics that we use data science to infer, versus the directly, observed metrics, like concessions, concession percentage.
Jake, hopefully you've discovered this, but we did recently add concession history as well for properties.
So you're able to easily come in.
And see the history of that property, as well as its comps.
You can do this on a value basis or percentage of rent, and then you can see the specific, concession that's offered, and the beds that it directly impacts.
One, … Wow.
Go.
One just quick thing on, concessions, and I did see that that feature was added.
So oftentimes what we're kind of doing is, we'll pull down the Excel export.
And then, we'll take a look at the, … we'll take a look at the unit level data, and we'll kind of average up you know, leases, perhaps, that were signed in the last 12 months at a certain property.
And then, obviously, it gives you the, the net effective rent data, the concession data.
But sometimes when we're looking at that report, the concession that's listed for each unit Right?
Is, not reflected in the net effective rent.
So maybe it says a month free.
In the, kind of, concession.
You know, notes, but then the net effective rent is not… doesn't reflect that month free.
So that's just one thing that we've kind of been noticing as we're… as we're working with this.
Yep.
Interesting.
And it's a scenario where the concession at the… that we're collecting… Should be applying to that unit type or floor plan type.
I mean, doesn't that just reflect that they rented it without a concession?
Often, operators will put a concession on 2 units out of 5 in a floor plan, and… It's possible they didn't… Issue a concession for that unit.

Yeah, if there's… Jake, if there's any particular properties where you've seen this on that come to mind, we can go back and try to take a look in the account, but if there's any in particular that you want us to go QA, we'd love to dive in and get you an answer on any of those.

Okay, yeah, next time that we're, that we're kind of looking at a deal, and I see that come up, I'll let you know, and I can, you know, forward you the output that we receive, and… We can kind of dive into it.
Yeah, we'll take a close look, for sure.
… We'll go back now and take a look at your account and make sure that we're not missing anything.
Generally speaking, yes, it should lock in the concession at the time that the unit was removed from the website.
And it's a… If there is logic within the concession itself, so if it says it applies only to one beds and not twos, the system should detect that and apply it to only one beds.
And so those might be the only scenario where you might see, like, some uneven distribution of concessions within a given property.
But if we're missing it on particular units, we obviously want to know about that, so we'll go dive in and see what we can find.
And then, yeah, please share with us.
I know it's not always ideal to shoot us an email or whatever, but if you're seeing it, we'd love to fix it.

Sounds good, yeah, we're, we're, … We're doing, you know, rent comp surveys on a daily basis, so, you know, next time we kind of see that, we'll… We'll, we'll shoot you guys an email, and we can see what's going on.
That'd be great, yeah, would love that.
You also mentioned renovated units.
Have you… have you used the filters as they exist today, Jake, and how have… how's that been playing out so far?
So, you know, kind of as… as I mentioned, … Typically.
Typically what we're doing is we're exporting all this data into Excel, and then kind of inserting it into our financial model.

And then we're kind of manipulating the data there, and … Like you said, you know, sometimes the renovated units are denoted in the floor plan code, and in those cases, you know, they're pretty easy to pick out, and … You know, we're seeing that on the… on the comp survey, but obviously at a lot of properties, it's not necessarily… Easy to ascertain on the website, if it's renovated or not.
And so, you know, we end up having to call the property or do some other kind of research to figure that out.
Yep.
Got it.

As we're working that, actually, our approach… our initial approach to this is going to be to really start to look for those price premiums, and then, of course, any amenity difference, but really try to infer based on price differences within an apples-to-apples floor plan.

Is there any way else that you might want to approach using this in IQ or in Excel that would make it easier for you to say, okay, I want to… this is going to be easier for me to tag particular units as being renovated?
Yeah, I mean, I think, … I think… … just… just any way that we can… denote them.
Obviously it's, … You know, not always gonna be publicly available.
… Whether or not, you know, units are renovated, or to what scope.
Because obviously we're looking, you know, a lot of times we're looking at properties where there's 4 or 5 different renovation scopes.
… So, you know, that becomes a little… a little tricky to… Kind of break everything up if it's not clearly labeled on the website, so….
I mean… Yep.
Obviously, something that would be helpful to us, and curious, … You know, how… you can kind of solve for those things without… Yep.
Without, kind of, having the information on hand.
For sure.

Yeah, we've got some, we've got some thesis that we think are gonna work, but, … Why don't we… we'll continue to take a run at that over the next few weeks here, and then reach out to you, just depending on how things are progressing, and make sure that it's getting you what you need.
Final thing you mentioned, Jake, is whether the comp's on rev management or not.
It sounds like this would be useful for you all as you're… as you're taking a look at the property itself.
Yeah, I mean, I think, … you know, I think… some… most, not all, properties that we're gonna be looking at are gonna be using revenue management.

… And… you know, I think our kind of… thought process is if a property is not using revenue management, like, for example, a lease up… a lot of times, lease ups won't be using revenue management because they're trying to get the… The property leased up, and they're giving big concessions, and so maybe their rents are being kind of held artificially high.
So, it's good to know that, just because, it just kind of gives some context to….
How they're pricing their rents.
Got it.

So it sounds like the… The starting point of that is really being able to confirm on a lease up whether or not there is revenue management being used, or there's… they're just sort of using the pro forma they gave to the banks, and sticking to that, and filling… backfilling with concessions.
Exactly, and there are other, you know, there are certain property managers that don't use it for one reason or another, so it's just kind of helpful to understand who's using it and who's not.
Got it.
Yeah, that's an easy one for us to add as well.

you know, we've got all the unit changes, so, you can always see within our unit table, how many price changes they've had, how many price changes that unit has had, over the course of while it's been on market.

So, if I just pull available units here… … So this is a, … If you see a number of rent changes, and they've been on the market for a number of days, you can infer it, and that's how we'll back into whether or not they're on revenue management anyway.
And then, of course, I know you're working primarily in Excel.
But you can also get a sense from these units of, the unit-specific price history as well.
Okay.
Got it, thanks.
Yeah, look forward to following up with you all on that.
Troy, why don't we, … I think what we're hoping to show you today is a new component of ApartmentIQ.
So we've had this Explore tab.
within IQ for a while now.
And it's been sort of a basic approach to market research.
But over the last 18 months or so, you know, ApartmentIQ has become a very popular product.
We've got about 5 million units now utilizing ApartmentIQ every day for all the use cases you all are using it for, and we appreciate you all being customers and partners to us.
As well.
And one of the requests we've had, we've had two major requests from our customers.
One is, what I think we're gonna talk to Charlotta about a little bit later, which is revenue management.
And using all of our public data to build a new revenue management approach.
And the second is, how do I use your daily data to start to, displace maybe some of the legacy, providers, like a RealPage Market Analytics, Yardi Matrix, CoStar, that tends to be call center data?
With the core… the core pain point being that data can sometimes get out of date.
Because it is call center data, and it often is a little bit less granular.
Because you've got to pick up the phone and get the occupancy, or the leasing, percentage, or how many leases have been signed, and so you're not going to get the unit-by-unit, breakdown there.
And so we've been building out and investing a fair amount into building out both those products.
And I want to give you a sense of where we're at today with this upgraded version of Explore.
So, actually, just this week, we started pushing rent growth forecasts into the product.
So I just pulled up Dallas.
I know you all, I was just kind of looking at a distribution of your portfolio.
It doesn't look like you're too heavy in Dallas, but I'll just pull this up and we can go to any market you want.
But the first thing you'll notice here is that we are looking at rent growth.
We've got these high-level metrics for the market, trailing 12, rent growth, what that price per unit is.
And then occupancy rate as well.

… And so, each of these items, you can filter by, we've built out a more robust filtering system, so you can decide if you want to only look at, property, you know, what the vintage of the property is, the size and units, the class of that property.
Obviously, there are variances in occupancy and rent growth across classes.

We've been building out a more robust property type filtering, which now includes what we think is, you know, the first definitive BTR database in the industry, that'll enable you to filter out or filter in whichever types of properties you might like.
As you get into certain markets, you can also decide if you only want to see certain properties managed by a particular group, or owned by a particular group.

And then you can also, decide if you want to just filter in specific properties that may be underperforming or overperforming the market using whatever metric might be interesting to you, occupancy, concessions, rent and NER, etc.
So, any of… these are all filterable, but I'll just start with the high level.
So, you can see this is the current rent growth forecast we have.
Going into DFW.
So this is on a quarterly basis, you can also look at this on a monthly basis, if you'd like.

We do have a new team with us, that we actually brought over from CoStar, so CoStar's forecasting team, and the primary forecaster for CoStar for the last decade now works here at ApartmentIQ, and these forecasts are coming from the same team, but with a slightly updated methodology.
And so, that team, we've seen opportunities to use newer forms of data science, to be able to generate these forecasts.
And the other side of this is largely.
our data just, … we have more up-to-date data.
So, in general, we should see these markets move faster than a legacy provider who's using call center data, and so we want to make sure that finds its way into our forecasts as well.

So, trying to piece, you know, try to really combine the multifamily knowledge that's driven a lot of the, kind of, consensus forecasts over the years with new approaches to forecasting and new approaches to data science, and of course, our ApartmentIQ data that's a bit more fresh and more granular.
Looks like you can, … there's a drop-down menu there that says rent.
What are some of the other options to forecast?
Oh, no.
Okay.
Yeah, net effective… right now, we're just forecasting asking rents.
Would net effective forecasts be valuable to you?
Yeah, I mean, it's, you know, it's making a… estimate on concessions going forward, right?
And so… Right.
… As long as it's, you know.
It's not going to be accurate, but, you know, uses the right methods to kind of forecast the concessions.
Yeah, I think that would be helpful.
Okay.
Ultimately, net effective is more important than market rent.
We really don't care about market rent, we just want to know what's going to happen with net effective.
Got it.
Well, we do see… obviously, we're collecting a lot of concessions within these markets, so we do see those trends.
It would be a new form of forecasting, but I'm sure the team would be excited to pick it up.
Yeah.
Just to give you some other, quick insights into what this looks like, so we have, we've layered new supply into the product.
This has been available in Explore.
We're gonna add the historical view of this as well, over the next week or so, so you'll be able to see what that trend looks like.
And this is obviously factoring into our forecast.
And then our demographics data as well, which has been in the product.
But you can always drill in and get the specific demographic details.
Additional section.
on an MSA level, right?
It's….
This is currently on an MSA level, yes, but you can do it, I should have mentioned, just for Dallas, for instance, you can do city level, county level, neighborhood level.
Huh.
As well.
And then you can always, if you'd like, I just randomly clicked a sub-market there, it looks like.
But you can also create custom, views as well.
So you can use our polygon tool to connect the dots, you can use a radius around a given point or property, and then you can also freehand it if you'd like.
Good.
And all this demographic data goes down to, functionally, the block, the city block.
And for those, do you get, … do you get rent growth forecasts?
That are generated by that, you know, sub-market.
The rent growth forecast that we're rolling out right now will go to the city and zip code level currently.
Okay.
Does that match, typically, how you all are looking for them?

Yeah, generally we're pulling… I mean, Matrix provides it on… you can call it a city, it's a sub-market, but, you know, if it's Richardson, Texas, it'll pull that'll be a sub-market of Dallas, and so we're able to pull it from Matrix on a sub-market basis.
Sometimes it's not necessarily city lines, so it could be, you know, … you know, Reno North, or Reno South, or something like that for a smaller market.
But, yeah, generally on a sub-market basis.
Do you all have a philosophy on what's more valuable in terms of even below that city level to a, you know, a true sub-market versus, like, a zip code?
I know that the, you know, approaches vary here.
How do you all like to approach that?
Personally, I like the sub-market, just because, you know, you have different supply… you may have huge supply in a city, in an MSA, but it may be concentrated in a certain sub-market.
So that's why I like to look at the sub-market information.
Okay.
And you gave Richardson as an example of that.
Any other particular sub-markets that are useful for you all?
Well, I say Richardson just because that's… we just closed on our first deal in… in DF… Metro, and it was… is in Richardson, so….
Oh, nice.
That was just an example, but, … Yeah, any…
any sub-market.

You know, for our existing portfolio, we are in the middle of budget season for 2026, and trying to, you know, pull different sources to project what kind of rent growth we should expect, and Currently, we're pulling that on a sub-market basis.
Got it.
Okay, yeah, so we'll be rolling that out.

Right now, we're expanding to the MSA, so we're going to be across 150 MSAs by early next week in the rent growth forecasting, and then we're gonna go… we're gonna take the forecast down over the next week or two beyond that.
So, happy to keep you all updated as those get rolled out into the product.
And sorry, I was kind of cutting out there, … Two questions, … how often do your rent growth forecasts get updated?
And then, second question is, what is the source of your demographic data, and how often is that updated?
Yep.
So that… We're, currently deciding exactly how frequently we want to update the rent forecast.
Right now, it's monthly.
But we think we could do it more frequently than that, if it would be useful.
How does that fit in with how you all might be able to… To get some health out of it.
I, I think, … I, you know… I think generally just a kind of… Regular cadence is helpful, so that we can… so we know when we should be kind of looking for updated forecasts.
… You know, where we get kind of tripped up is… When the frequency of forecast updates is kind of sporadic.
Got it.
Perfect.
Good feedback.
Yeah, I would imagine… we're expecting it's going to be pretty consistent, at least a monthly, potentially more frequent basis.

and I'm not, … I haven't really seen reports that look like this, but, just kind of thinking, like.
maybe if there was a way to back-select, a forecast for any particular MSA or sub-market that you could have been looking at.

It sounds like.

You don't want to overfit to, you know, what's happened in the past, but one of the ways we certainly judge the accuracy of the models before we deploy them is How well they would have predicted shifts in the past, and then we're also calibrating, against what we view as sort of the consensus, or each source's view on what the growth rates are.
So, to the extent that we see differences in our model, we certainly want to dive in and see what's driving that, and whether we think we're right or not.

Obviously, measures of rent growth can be.
Different, ….
based on how you're cutting out the sub-market, you know, the data provider, how the data's being collected, etc.

So, kind of having a same store Here's what we forecast at this time, and here's what actually occurred, and that way we can kind of see, you know, how the forecast lined up with what actually happened on an apples-to-apples basis.

And my, … I guess the other, I guess, challenge we've had with forecasts and… Again, the explainability and the timing of it is, for instance, many forecasts get changed on very short timelines and unpredictable timelines, and without any explanation.
And so, as an example, we use forecasts, when we're, you know, deciding whether or not to buy a property.
We decide that we want to, you know, bid a certain price for a property.
We get awarded that property, we're 2 weeks into due diligence, 5 weeks after the last update, and all of a sudden, rent growth forecasts go from 4% to 1.
5%, and then it's like, what do we do with that?
Right, and there's no explanation for it.
So that's just an example of, like, the frustration that we have with Yeah.
Not having any context, and having frequent and highly variable changes.

seem to be just about right for on target for what the goal for this property is.

I'm curious, I know this is the first time showing this to you, Charlotta, but one of the goals for our revenue management product was to, be able to show, you know, really explain every recommendation we're making.
I'm curious how this resonates with you.
I like that it… It's not, you know, it's not a black box, you can actually see why it's recommending a decrease.
How does it decide how big of a decrease to recommend, though?

Yeah, so… Functionally, what it does is, it takes it, and it looks, okay, so we're… at the current prices, we're expecting a decrease, you know, we're expecting a lease deficit, and then it kicks… it looks at a price elasticity curve.

That is actually not shown currently in the product, but we could show it to you, which basically factors in all the leases that have been signed at this property, as well as all the leases that have been signed in the market.
And it calculates how many leases you will gain or lose based on the change in price.
So that price elasticity curve, is what's… is what's informing the… that almost $20 decrease.
